does: This paper introduces ChangeQuery, a multimodal framework for post-disaster damage assessment that moves beyond pixel-level change detection to semantic understanding of disaster impacts. The work constructs the Disaster-Induced Change Query (DICQ) dataset, which pairs optical imagery from before disasters with synthetic aperture radar (SAR) data from after events, covering both natural disasters and armed conflicts. The framework incorporates an Automated Semantic Annotation Pipeline to generate high-quality training labels. The system enables interactive, complex semantic queries about disaster damage rather than simple binary change detection, operating across varying weather conditions through its multimodal approach.
connects: This work extends the progression from traditional change detection methods toward vision-language models for remote sensing analysis. It builds on recent advances in multimodal learning that combine optical and SAR data for disaster monitoring, addressing known limitations in existing approaches that rely solely on optical imagery and struggle with all-weather capability. The paper responds to growing recognition that disaster response requires semantic understanding beyond what pixel-level or unimodal approaches provide. It engages with both the natural disaster assessment literature and the emerging application of remote sensing to conflict analysis, bridging these traditionally separate research communities while inheriting techniques from annotation-efficient learning and interactive AI systems.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward truly interactive disaster response systems where emergency managers can query specific damage scenarios and receive actionable intelligence. The multimodal foundation combining optical and SAR data establishes a more robust baseline for all-weather disaster monitoring that could generalize to cloud-covered regions and nighttime scenarios. The unified treatment of natural and human-induced disasters suggests future work on domain-agnostic disaster understanding. The emphasis on semantic grounding and interactive reasoning points toward systems that can answer nuanced questions about infrastructure damage patterns, humanitarian needs, and prioritization for resource allocation, transforming remote sensing from observational to decisional intelligence in crisis contexts.
